# System Architecture

*Generated by Tech Lead on 2026-03-13T09:56:58.546Z*

## ⚠️ SOURCE OF TRUTH

This document is the **authoritative source** for all technical decisions.
All team members (Maker, Reviewer, Coordinator) MUST follow this architecture.
Any deviation requires approval from the Tech Lead.

---

## Tech Stack Selection

**Justification:** TypeScript across client and server reduces integration errors and improves developer productivity. React + Vite yields a fast UI development loop for the product listing/cart interface. Node + Express is lightweight and easy to iterate for an MVP backend; Prisma provides a typed DB schema and smooth migration path from SQLite (fast local dev) to PostgreSQL (production). Vitest and Playwright satisfy the testing requirements (unit + UI smoke). A mock payment adapter supports deterministic CI and local testing; Stripe test mode provides a production-like payment integration when needed.

- **Languages:** TypeScript, SQL

- **Frameworks:** React (client), Vite (frontend dev/build), Node.js + Express (server), Prisma (ORM / DB layer)

- **Databases:** SQLite (development), PostgreSQL (production)

- **Tools:** Vitest (unit tests), Playwright (UI smoke/e2e), ESLint, Prettier (lint/format), pnpm or npm (package manager), GitHub Actions (CI), Stripe (test mode) or a mock payment adapter

---

## Component Architecture

The application is a single-page client (React) that communicates with a RESTful backend (Express). Frontend components (ProductList, ProductCard, Cart, CartItem, CheckoutForm, Header) manage UI state and call backend endpoints for authoritative data and persistent cart operations. Cart state is kept locally for immediate responsiveness but is synchronized with the backend via cart endpoints to survive reloads and support multi-device later. The backend provides stable endpoints: GET /api/products, GET /api/products/:id, GET /api/cart, POST /api/cart (add/update), DELETE /api/cart/items/:id, POST /api/checkout. Business logic—price calculation, quantity validation, and order composition—lives on the backend to prevent client-side tampering.

Payment flow is abstracted behind a payment adapter interface with two implementations: a deterministic mock adapter (used in dev/CI/Playwright) and a Stripe-compatible adapter (used in sandbox/production). Tests target the core pricing and cart logic independently (unit tests) and exercise the full UI flow (Playwright) using the mock adapter. This separation ensures the arithmetic and business rules are verifiable without UI flakiness and that the end-to-end purchase flow can be validated deterministically in CI.

## Directory Structure

```
package.json
pnpm-lock.yaml (or package-lock.json)
tsconfig.json
/.github/workflows/ci.yml
/README.md
/RELEASE.md
/DOCS/PLANNING.md
/client/

  vite.config.ts
  index.html
  src/
    main.tsx
    App.tsx
    api.ts                # small fetch wrappers for /api
    /components/
      Header.tsx
      ProductList.tsx
      ProductCard.tsx
      Cart.tsx
      CartItem.tsx
      CheckoutForm.tsx
    /styles/
      app.css
    /tests/
      ui.playwright.spec.ts
  public/
    assets/ (images, icons)
/server/
  package.json
  prisma/
    schema.prisma
  src/
    server.ts
    routes/
      products.ts
      cart.ts
      checkout.ts
    services/
      productService.ts
      cartService.ts
      paymentAdapter.ts     # interface + factory
      mockPaymentAdapter.ts
      stripeAdapter.ts      # stub (implements interface)
    prisma/
      client.ts
      seed.ts
    utils/
      money.ts              # price math helpers
      errors.ts
    tests/
      unit/
        cart.test.ts
        pricing.test.ts
/tests/
  ui/
    checkout.spec.ts
/assets/
  product thumbnails, icons
/DOCS/
  ARCHITECTURE.md
  API.md
```

---

## Security Strategy

Treat payments and customer interactions carefully even in MVP. Never store raw payment credentials—use tokenized payment flows (Stripe) or a mock adapter for tests. Keep secrets (API keys, DB credentials) in environment variables or a secret manager and do not commit them. Validate and sanitize all incoming data server-side (product IDs, quantities, payment tokens). Use parameterized DB access via Prisma to prevent injection. Enforce CORS policy in production to restrict allowed origins and add basic rate-limiting on sensitive endpoints (checkout) to reduce abuse. For local CI and smoke tests, prefer the mock payment adapter to avoid real payment calls; when using Stripe test mode, use test keys stored only in CI secrets. Serve the application over HTTPS in production and run dependency audits regularly.

---

## Error Handling Strategy

Centralize error handling on the server with typed error classes (ValidationError, NotFoundError, PaymentError, InternalError). Routes should catch errors and return structured JSON { error: { code, message } } with appropriate HTTP status codes (4xx for client issues, 5xx for server problems). Log full stack traces server-side (to console or a logging service) while returning minimal, user-friendly messages to clients. For payment failures return clear recovery actions (e.g., 'Payment declined — try another card') and retain a record of failed attempts for diagnostics. On the client, map server error codes to UI states and show actionable messages; use an Error Boundary to catch render-time exceptions and provide a graceful reload or reset path. Ensure tests assert on known error codes/messages to detect regressions without exposing internals.
